message:
fix(datetime): accept RFC3339 Z/offset timezone suffixes in parse_iso8601

julianday() and the other §13.3 datetime functions rejected RFC3339
timestamps bearing a Z, ±HH:MM, ±HHMM, or ±HH timezone suffix because
parse_iso8601 never consumed anything after the time part and
parse_time_part choked on the non-numeric trailing characters.  That
blocked bounded downstream patterns like:

    CREATE INDEX ix ON event_log(julianday(created_at));

whenever created_at stored RFC3339 timestamps — e.g. any Rust app
using chrono::DateTime::to_rfc3339, Go's time.RFC3339, or Python's
isoformat().

This change splits the timezone suffix off before running the numeric
parse path, reads an offset in minutes east of UTC, and subtracts it
from the resulting JDN so that the stored value matches SQLite's
canonical "everything in UTC" interpretation.  The offset grammar
accepts:

  Z | z               (zero, UTC)
  ±HH:MM              (ISO-8601 extended)
  ±HHMM               (ISO-8601 basic)
  ±HH                 (hours-only short form)

Hours must be 0..=23 and minutes 0..=59; malformed offsets fall
through to the existing NULL-returning error path rather than
silently truncating.

Added nine regression tests covering the Z suffix (upper/lower
case), +00:00 / -00:00, positive/negative full-hour offsets, the
+05:30 half-hour case, the compact ±HHMM / ±HH forms, fractional
seconds combined with a TZ suffix, a full date()/time()/datetime()
round-trip that verifies the UTC normalization is visible through
all output functions, and a deliberate-malformed-offset case that
must return NULL.  All numeric expectations match the C SQLite
reference output reported in the issue.

Closes #64.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
